pages.full_text:
The dog poo in Millan is appalling. Simon trod in a really soupy one. Apparently poo + pee are used in leather work, so perhaps they've used to it. A pisserwoman used to go out every morning to collect contributions from every house hold. And I think the french read that dog shit was brought in from Islambul, Constantinople.

Millau + Grenoble were important glove-making towns. But when gloves went out of fashion in the 1950s, they lost jobs. There was a film of glove-making explained by a "Gantier". All his family had been glove makers too. A lot of leather-stretching was involved.

We drove on to Entraygues after lunch in a field by a river. There were huge brushwood stacks up in the trees by the river from winter flooding. It's hot again. Charles paddled across the river.

The Entraygues campsite is called __Val dos Saures__ (Valley of the lizards?) *+** pitch 104
one of the best campsites we've ever been in
pages.notes: The writer spells 'Millau' as 'Millan' in the first paragraph and 'Millau' in the second. 'Islambul' is the diary's spelling (for Istanbul/Constantinople). 'Gantier' likely means 'Gantier' (glove-maker in French). Marginalia on right edge of page reads 'Note p.33: Clipper Bridge / p.38 Garabit' — appears to be a cross-reference annotation. 'Val dos Saures' is underlined; '(Valley of the lizards?)' is the diarist's own translation. '*+**' and 'pitch 104' appear to be campsite rating symbols and pitch number added in blue ink. 'Entraygues' may be Entraygues-sur-Truyère in Aveyron, France, consistent with the Millau/Grenoble context.

pages.full_text: It's rather annoying that the men at the Accueil take the hours off 12-4, which means that if you get there at 1.45, you don't know for ages if there's a space.
pages.notes: Page is dominated by a glued-in colour photograph of a modern cable-stayed or arch bridge, viewed from below/side — likely the Millau Viaduct or a similar landmark bridge in the Aveyron region. Marginalia in gutter (visible in both images) reads 'Note p.33: Clipper Bridge / p.38 Garabit' — cross-reference annotation, attributed to left page. Text below the photo continues the campsite narrative from the left page. 'Accueil' is the French word for reception/welcome desk.
